preamble: Shift of labiodental fricative [f] to bilabial fricative [ɸ], representing bilabialization of fricatives. Ewe (Kwa, Niger-Congo) maintains a phonemic contrast between labiodental fricatives /f, v/ and their bilabial counterparts /ɸ, β/, a rare feature in world languages.

examples.note: Ewe contrasts labiodental fricatives /f, v/ with bilabial fricatives /ɸ, β/, one of few languages with this distinction. The bilabial variant represents an articulatory variation or allophonic realization in certain phonological contexts, or may be a separate phoneme depending on analysis. Both are written distinctly in Ewe orthography (f vs. ƒ).
In Japanese, the voiceless fricative before /u/ is realized as bilabial [ɸu] rather than labio-dental [fu], representing a natural phonological interaction with the rounded vowel.

sources: Ladefoged, P., & Maddieson, I. (1996). The Sounds of the World's Languages.
